world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Puzzle board display text ('WHEEL OF FORTUNE') shown during game setup phase
Wheel wedge values displayed on spinning wheel; different monetary values ($1,000 max in standard game) indicate round tier
Coca-Cola logo intended for title screen scrolling text (unused in final build); palette allocation suggests formal brand integration

environmental_storytelling.example: Top dollar amount of $1,000 establishes economic scope and reward tier expectations; unused higher values ($2,000-$10,000) indicate development-stage iteration on show authenticity

Puzzle 'FIRST DAY ON THE JOB' unreachable in final rotation suggests backend logic constraints; puzzle content (Bob Dylan lyric vs. Dangerfield catchphrase) changed between revisions reflecting cultural reference updates
Coca-Cola logo graphical assets embedded in VRAM for title and copyright screens; removal between revisions correlates with MGE acquisition timeline (1988 Columbia Pictures sale)

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Displays monetary wedges; values from supported range (IDs 0C-35, B4-B5); glitched rendering on certain high-value spaces lacking proper digit graphics
Displays setup text; transitions to active puzzle state
Puzzle content swapped between Rev 0 and Rev 1 (Dylan reference → Dangerfield catchphrase); suggests content refresh cycle

notes: Wheel of Fortune (NES, 1988) is a faithful adaptation of the television game show with minimal environmental world-state complexity. The game communicates primarily through game-show mechanical elements (wheel values, puzzle categories) rather than faction dynamics or danger states. Notable manifestation patterns: (1) Wheel wedges encode monetary reward hierarchy visually; (2) Unused wedge IDs indicate development-stage feature parity attempts with daytime/nighttime TV show variants; (3) Coca-Cola branding removal reflects real-world corporate event (MGE acquisition), showing how external world-state affected game manifestation; (4) Puzzle content revision suggests iterative refinement of cultural relevance. No danger/safety signals, faction systems, or political states documented—appropriate for puzzle-game paradigm. Data heavily weighted toward technical/asset layer rather than experiential manifestation.
